description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 59-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted with a progressively enlarging but painless mass in the region of the umbilicus, which developed two months after liver transplantation. The mass is approximately 5 cm in diameter, firm with irregular borders, wine-red shade, and fixed to surrounding tissues.

Physical examination:

* Overlying skin not ulcerated
* Gynecological history unremarkable
* No smoking or alcohol use

Vital signs and basic laboratory results:

* Hemoglobin level: 9.6 g/dl
* Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dl
* Conjugated bilirubin: 0.1 mg/dl
* Alkaline Phosphates: 273 U/l
* GGT: 40 U/L
* AST: 36 U/L
* ALT: 56 U/L

Imaging:

* Plain anterior/posterior chest x-ray: normal
* Ultrasound of the abdominal cavity:
	+ 4 × 4 cm mass below the umbilicus
	+ Additional smaller nodules confined to the lower abdominal wall
	+ No paraaortic lymph nodes identified

seed_nodes.claim: progressively enlarging but painless mass in the region of the umbilicus

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
**Diagnostic Challenge**

A 23-year-old patient presents with a single cutaneous abscess on the right side of the neck, cervical lymphadenopathy, and axillary lymphadenopathy. The patient is HIV-negative and has been on immunosuppressive therapy for polymyositis.

**Initial Presentation:**

* Cutaneous abscess on the right side of the neck
* Cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy

**Physical Exam:**

* Normal examination of other systems

**Lab Results:**

* Sputum samples negative for AFB in three consecutive spot-early morning-spot samples
* Pus from cutaneous abscess sent for culture and further analysis

seed_nodes.claim: cutaneous abscess on the right side of the neck

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 75-year-old diabetic male presents with a one-week history of dull upper abdominal pain and swelling in the left upper abdomen. He denies vomiting, fever, or gastrointestinal bleeding.

Physical examination reveals a 14 × 10 cm mass palpable in the epigastrium and left hypochondrium with minimal intrinsic mobility.

Routine biochemical investigations are normal. Ultrasonogram and CT-scan of the abdomen show a large heterogeneous mass (13 × 10 cm) extending from the tail of pancreas to anterior pararenal space, lesser sac to gastrosplenic ligament enveloping the posterior aspect of fundus, body, and greater curvature.

One day after admission, examination shows reduction in size of palpable mass to 8 × 6 cm without aggravation of symptoms. Ultrasonography repeats this finding with a reduced diameter of mass (8 × 8 cm).

Upper endoscopy reveals a large bulge in fundus and corpus of the stomach posteriorly with an opening in the posterior part of the corpus, demonstrating dissemination of serous fluid and necrotic material into the stomach. Fluid analysis is normal for CEA and CA 19-9.

What is the next step in this patient's evaluation?

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the surgical clinic with a three-month history of epigastric pain, feculent vomiting, and post-prandial diarrhea. She has lost over one stone in weight. Her past medical history is unremarkable, except for an investigation two years prior for intermittent left-sided abdominal pain, which was attributed to irritable bowel syndrome.

Physical examination reveals a healthy appearing patient with no significant findings on abdominal examination.

Initial laboratory results include:

* Haemoglobin: 9.5 g/dl
* Mean cell volume: 71.6 fl
* White cell count: 20.2 × 10^9/l
* Urea, electrolytes, and liver function tests are all normal

An ultrasound scan of the abdomen shows thickened bowel in the right upper quadrant with a dilated duodenum. A barium meal and follow-through demonstrates a mucosal abnormality on the greater curvature of the stomach with a fistulous tract into the transverse colon (Figure ). Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy confirms the presence of a mucosal abnormality on the greater curvature of the stomach, but initial biopsies are negative for malignancy.

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Pulmonary embolism is not mentioned in the case, but I found another potential diagnosis that matches your request:

 Enlarging sclerotic lesion in the thoracic spine 

However, since the text describes it as "an enlarging sclerotic lesion" and later mentions a CT-guided biopsy was performed to confirm its nature, I will provide the alternative diagnosis:

Spinal metastasis
vignette:
**Diagnostic Challenge**

A 55-year-old woman with a significant smoking history presents to your clinic. She has a history of recurrent bronchioloalveolar carcinoma, including two previous surgeries for localized disease.

**Initial Clinical Presentation**

She complains of general malaise and fatigue.

**Physical Exam**

Vital signs are stable, but the patient appears tired and short of breath. Lungs are clear to auscultation, with no evidence of wheezing or rales.

**First-Line Labs/Imaging**

A CT scan of the chest shows innumerable bilateral nodules. Basic lab work is pending; you have ordered a complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel (BMP). The patient's history of previous lung cancer surgeries and recurrent disease raises concerns for metastatic or recurrent disease, but the clinical presentation and imaging findings are subtle. What would be your next steps in evaluating this patient?

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 31-year-old man presents with a painless left inguinal tumor that has gradually grown over the past six months.

Physical examination reveals a fist-sized subcutaneous tumor in the inguinal region with normal-appearing overlying skin without ulceration, and no inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Lab/imaging results:

* Computed Tomography: shows a solid tumor with heterogeneous contrast enhancement in the adipose tissue
* No metastases to the liver and lung are seen on CT

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Pulmonary Embolism is NOT mentioned in the case.

 Dilated aortic root is a background condition.

Here's the acute, primary presenting diagnosis: 

 Dilated aortic root is a chronic, pre-existing condition, however:
 
 Complicated transseptal puncture and potentially fatal complications
vignette:
A 70-year-old male patient presents with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter, as well as concomitant arterial hypertension. The patient's initial symptoms are not specified.

Physical exam:

*   The examination findings are not described in the case report.

Lab results:

*   Initial lab results (e.g., complete blood count, basic metabolic panel) are not mentioned.
*   Electrocardiogram (ECG) results show paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter.

Imaging studies:

*   A transthoracic echocardiogram (TEE) was performed prior to the procedure, which described an aneurysm of the interatrial septum (IAS).
*   Intraprocedural intracardiac echocardiography (ICE) showed:
    *   Dilated aortic root
    *   Enlarged right atrium displacing the IAS towards the left atrial free wall
    *   Small oval fossa with flapping motion of the fossa ovalis membrane

seed_nodes.claim: paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
vignette:
A 33-year-old male presents to your clinic with a 3-month history of decreased vision in the left eye and a left-sided headache. The physical exam reveals left eye blindness with primary optic atrophy, but no other neurological deficits are noted.

What is the first step you would take in evaluating this patient?

Laboratory tests:

* What initial labs or imaging studies would you order to help diagnose this condition?
 
Imaging:

* A CT scan of the brain and orbits has been performed, revealing a bony tumor in the left ethmoid sinus with invasion into the left orbit and compression of the optic nerve. There is also intracranial extension into the anterior cranial fossa on the left side. What does this suggest about the possible diagnosis?

seed_nodes.claim: Decreased vision in the left eye

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Appendicitis
Pulmonary embolism is not present in this case, but rather bilateral pleural effusions are mentioned.
The correct answer is Appendicitis.

However, it's worth noting that the patient also had an intrathoracic hernia which complicated the diagnosis and treatment. 

But since you asked for the ACUTE primary presenting diagnosis, I will stick to your request.

Appendicitis
vignette:
**Diagnostic Challenge**

A 43-year-old female district nurse presents to the emergency department with:

* A 6-hour history of generalized abdominal pain that has localized to the right
* Associated nausea and loss of appetite, made worse by movement and not relieved by intramuscular morphine injection
* No recent or past trauma to the chest or abdomen

On examination, she is tender in the right upper quadrant and right iliac fossa with:

* Rebound and guarding
* Rovsing positive sign
* Normal bowel sounds

She has a positive family history of Marfan's syndrome but had not undergone genetic testing. Notable physical examination findings include:

* Long span of upper limbs
* High-arched palate
* Soft early diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation

Initial lab results and imaging include:

* Pyrexia
* Raised white cell count
* Elevated C-reactive protein
* Abdominal X-ray showing absence of gas in the right side of her bowel
* Chest X-ray showing no obvious abnormality

What is your differential diagnosis based on this clinical presentation, physical exam findings, and initial lab results/imaging?

seed_nodes.claim: Generalized abdominal pain that has localized to the right

description: Real world case report from PMC (Scrubbed)
target_diagnosis:
Here is the acute, primary presenting diagnosis:

* Acute cholecystitis 
* Biliary colic
vignette:
A 32-year-old female presents with a 3-hour history of epigastric pain radiating into her back, consistent with biliary colic. She has vomited several times and had similar episodes in the previous week.

On examination, she is found to have no jaundice or pyrexia. The apex beat is located in the right fifth intercostal space, midclavicular line. There is epigastric tenderness but no tenderness in the right or left upper quadrants.

Laboratory results show a normal white cell count and amylase level, but an elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 290 mg/L. An electrocardiograph reveals right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy, consistent with dextrocardia.

An ultrasound scan of the upper abdomen identifies the gallbladder in the left upper quadrant, containing stones, and a spleen located in the right upper quadrant. There is no evidence of common bile duct or intrahepatic duct dilatation on the ultrasound. A chest X-Ray confirms the presence of dextrocardia.

What are your differential diagnoses for this patient's presentation?
